did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used supervised classification (machine learning) to assess Asymmetry, Border, and Color criteria and Overall Risk of pigmented skin lesions. Specifically, it employed Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Bayes classifiers combined with feature selection methods (W+LFS, F+CFS, F+ReliefF) to classify mobile-acquired skin lesion images based on the ABCD rule.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an image processing and analysis methodology for pigmented skin lesions using mobile devices. It describes a specific dataset of 80 mobile-acquired images, details the methodology (segmentation, feature extraction, feature selection, and classification using SVM and Bayes classifiers), and reports original results including accuracy rates (73.8%, 76.7%, 68.8%) and sensitivity/specificity (86%, 73%). Although it references prior work and reviews in the introduction, the core contribution is a new technical study with empirical validation, not a review of existing literature.

title: Pigmented Skin Lesion Computerized Analysis via Mobile Devices
brief_summary: This study proposes a computerized analysis methodology for pigmented skin lesions using images acquired via mobile devices to support early melanoma detection. The researchers developed a supervised classification system based on the ABCD rule to independently assess Asymmetry, Border, and Color criteria, as well as Overall Risk, using Support Vector Machines and Bayes classifiers. The methodology was evaluated on a dataset of 80 mobile-acquired images manually annotated by a medical specialist. Feature extraction involved 1069 features per image, followed by feature selection techniques to optimize classification performance. The system achieved accuracy rates of 73.8%, 76.7%, and 68.8% for Asymmetry, Border, and Color criteria, respectively. Additionally, the automatic Overall Risk assessment demonstrated 86% sensitivity and 73% specificity.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on a dataset of 80 pigmented skin lesions (skin moles) acquired via mobile devices. The lesions were annotated for risk assessment (Low/High Overall Risk) and ABCD criteria (Asymmetry, Border, Color). While Malignant Melanoma is mentioned as the primary clinical concern motivating the study, the specific dataset and classification tasks were not limited to a binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma distinction but rather assessed risk scores and criteria for pigmented lesions in general.

Malignant Melanoma is cited as the most dangerous form of skin cancer and the primary motivation for the study. However, the paper does not report a specific classification task distinguishing melanoma from other lesions in the 80-image dataset; instead, it uses 'Overall Risk' (Low/High) as a proxy for malignancy potential.

all_task_types.target: Asymmetry score (0-2), Border score (0-8), Color score (1-3), and Overall Risk (Low/High)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses supervised classification (Naive Bayes and SVM) to predict discrete categorical scores for the ABCD rule criteria (Asymmetry, Border, Color) and a binary Overall Risk label (Low/High) for pigmented skin lesions. The targets are discrete classes defined by the ABCD rule, not continuous values.

datasets_used.name: Mobile-acquired skin lesion dataset (Project Melanoma Detection)
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 80 color images (652x652 pixels) of pigmented skin lesions acquired using an HTC One S mobile phone. The images were collected at the Portuguese Institute of Oncology of Porto under the scope of the project Melanoma Detection. All images were manually annotated by a medical specialist regarding Asymmetry, Border, and Color scores (ABCD rule) and Overall Risk (Low/High), and manually segmented to serve as ground truth.

use.training: Used for training classifiers (SVM, Bayes) via leave-one-out cross-validation, where for each classification all images except the one being classified were used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed methodology via leave-one-out cross-validation to evaluate sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.
use.other: Used to manually segment ground truth for evaluating segmentation accuracy (True Detection Rate).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the classification performance (sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy) of the SVM and Bayes classifiers on the dataset of 80 images.
Metric used to measure the percentage of lesions correctly classified as asymmetric, with abrupt border, or high risk.
Metric used to measure the percentage of lesions correctly classified as symmetric, with smooth border, or low risk.
Metric used to measure the overall percentage of lesions correctly classified.
Metric used to evaluate the segmentation methodology by measuring the rate of pixels classified as lesion by both the automatic method and the expert ground truth.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy via leave-one-out cross-validation.

Asymmetry.notes: The best result for Asymmetry was achieved using a Bayes classifier with the W+LFS feature selection method, utilizing 10 selected features.

Border.notes: The best result for Border was achieved using an SVM classifier with the W+LFS feature selection method, utilizing 9 selected features. Sensitivity was 81.4% and Specificity was 72.1%.

Color.notes: The best result for Color was achieved using an SVM classifier with the F+CFS feature selection method, utilizing 44 selected features. Sensitivity and Specificity were not applicable (n.a.) for this specific criterion in the reported best combination.

Overall Risk.notes: The best result for Overall Risk was achieved using an SVM classifier with the ReliefF filter-based method. This result was obtained with a subset of 30 features (13 from Asymmetry, 3 from Border, and 14 from Color criteria).

Segmentation.notes: The segmentation methodology achieved a mean True Detection Rate of 95.6% with 6.14% standard deviation when compared with ground truth images.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Naive Bayes classifiers

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 1069 features extracted per image (310 Asymmetry, 97 Border, 662 Color features) including geometry, color distribution, GLCM, GLRLM, and Wavelet features
architecture_details.svm_details: C-Support Vector Classification (C-SVC) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel
architecture_details.implementation: C++ and OpenCV library for segmentation/feature extraction; Weka software for feature selection

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: C-Support Vector Classification (C-SVC) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive segmentation algorithm based on Otsu's threshold method with dynamically estimated threshold parameters according to image pixel resolution
Manual segmentation by a medical specialist to serve as ground truth
Rotation of lesion images (as indicated in Figure 1 caption: 'Original rotated lesion')
Extraction of horizontal and vertical asymmetry masks based on principal and secondary axes of inertia
Division of border into 8 segments and classification of inner, border, and outer regions
Cluster-based color feature analysis (k=1 to 6 clusters)
conclusions: The proposed methodology using supervised classification can independently assess the Asymmetry, Border, and Color criteria according to the ABCD rule, as well as the Overall Risk, for pigmented skin lesions acquired via mobile devices.
Accuracy rates of 73.8%, 76.7%, and 68.8% were achieved for the estimation of the ABCD score of Asymmetry, Border, and Color criteria, respectively.
The automatic Overall Risk assessment achieved 86% sensitivity and 73% specificity.

The Differential Structures criterion was not assessed in this study because mobile-acquired images lack the necessary image quality (magnification and polarized illumination) to identify the required structures.
The segmentation approach used is optimized for the specific skin lesion image acquisition methodology described in prior work.
Validation of the suggested methodology requires a larger dataset of mobile-acquired images from different mobile phone brands, annotated by medical specialists.
Future work intends to use the proposed image processing module in a framework for risk triage of skin cancer using new generations of mobile devices.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small dataset of only 80 mobile-acquired images, which restricts the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
The dataset was collected from a single source (Portuguese Institute of Oncology of Porto) using a single mobile device model (HTC One S), introducing potential population and device-specific bias.

The 'Differential Structures' criterion of the ABCD rule was excluded because mobile-acquired images lack the necessary magnification (10x) and polarized illumination required to identify these structures, limiting the clinical applicability of the full ABCD rule.

The segmentation algorithm was specifically optimized for the image acquisition methodology of the HTC One S, raising concerns about its performance on images from other mobile devices or lighting conditions.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics.
External validation was not performed; the authors explicitly state that validation requires a larger dataset from different mobile phone brands annotated by medical specialists.
The classification performance for the Color criterion was relatively low (68.8% accuracy), and sensitivity/specificity were not reported for this specific criterion in the best-performing model.
Leave-one-out cross-validation was used on the small dataset, which can result in high variance in performance estimates.
